w:r.w:t: GrammarX: Development of a Web Application for Interactive English Grammar Learning
Figure 2. Host dashboard during a game
Figure 3. Player view in Duel Mode (G2)
Figure 4. QR code for room access

The accelerated advancement of digital technologies has profoundly transformed teaching and learning methods at all educational levels. In the field of language learning, gamification—that is, the incorporation of game elements and mechanics into educational contexts—has proven to be an effective strategy for increasing student motivation, active participation, and knowledge retention (Kapp, 2012). In this context, the present project proposes the development of GrammarX, a multiplayer interactive web game application designed specifically for learning and practicing English grammatical structures.

Platforms such as Kahoot!, Quizlet, and Duolingo have revolutionized the way students interact with educational content. Kahoot! (2013) introduced the model of competitive real-time quizzes in the classroom, achieving high levels of engagement and participation (Wang & Lieberoth, 2016). Quizlet offers flashcards and minigames for vocabulary learning, while Duolingo gamifies progressive language acquisition through short lessons and reward systems. However, these platforms present significant limitations for teachers who need to work on specific grammar topics: they do not allow customization of game mechanics according to each class's curricular objectives, and in many cases require paid subscriptions for advanced features. This gap motivated the development of a custom, adaptable, and open-source solution.
This project pursues the following objectives:
Develop an interactive web application that allows students to practice four English grammar structures: Past Simple, Comparatives and Superlatives, Present Perfect, and Future Tense.
Implement a real-time multiplayer game system that enables simultaneous participation of all students in a classroom.
Incorporate gamification mechanics (scoring, timer, leaderboard) to increase student motivation and engagement.
Provide the teacher with tools to control game sessions and real-time visibility of student performance.

GrammarX is a client-server web application that allows a teacher (host) to create a virtual room and invite students to join using a room code or QR code. Once assembled, the host selects one of four available minigames, configures the number of questions and time per round, and launches the game. Students compete in real time by answering grammar questions from their own mobile devices or computers. At the end of the game, a leaderboard is displayed with the results. The application is deployed on the Railway.app platform and is publicly accessible at https://grammarx-production.up.railway.app/
Based on the analysis of the educational context in which the application would be used, the following functional requirements were identified:
Creation and management of game rooms identified by a unique code.
Player join via room code or QR code scan.
Four minigames with differentiated mechanics for four grammar topics.
Synchronized per-round timer visible to all participants.
Scoring system that considers difficulty level and response speed.
Persistent leaderboard per minigame stored in the database.
Correct answer revealed only after all players respond or time expires.
Host dashboard with real-time player status.
Real-time communication with minimal latency via WebSockets.
Cross-platform compatibility: desktop and mobile browsers.
Mandatory HTTPS on local network to enable device camera access.
Deployable on cloud platforms without source code changes.
Responsive interface optimized for mobile screens.

GrammarX adopts a two-layer client-server architecture. The frontend is a Single Page Application (SPA) built with React 18 and compiled with Vite 5. The backend is a Node.js 24 server running Express.js to serve the static frontend files and Socket.io to manage real-time communication. Data persistence is implemented through the native node:sqlite API built into Node.js 24, which provides synchronous access to a local SQLite database without additional dependencies. In production, the server is deployed on Railway.app and operates in pure HTTP mode, as the platform handles TLS termination. In local environments, the server automatically generates a self-signed certificate via the selfsigned library, required for the browser to allow camera access (getUserMedia API) on devices connected to the classroom's local area network.
[Figure 1: Architecture diagram — React client, Node.js/Express server, Socket.io and SQLite database]
Figure 1. GrammarX home screen showing role selection (Host / Player).

The SQLite database contains four tables. The sessions table stores the state of each game session: code, status (waiting, playing, ended, closed), minigame identifier, rounds in JSON format, current round, round start timestamp, time per round, and a unique game token to prevent duplicate answers. The players table records which players belong to each session. The answers table stores responses submitted by each player, including the round index, score obtained, and game token. Finally, the scores table accumulates historical scores per player and minigame for the global leaderboard.

The GrammarX interface follows a dark theme inspired by the visual style of modern gaming platforms. JetBrains Mono is used as the primary typeface for its readability on low-resolution screens, complemented by Bricolage Grotesque for headings. The design is mobile-first, with a centered single-column layout that adapts to smartphone screens without additional adjustments. Accent colors vary according to the active minigame to provide immediate visual feedback to the user.

The frontend is implemented as a single root React component with states and effects managing transitions between screens (login, waiting room, game, results, leaderboard). Communication with the server is done exclusively through a singleton Socket.io socket initialized when the application loads. In production mode, the socket connects to the same server origin (window.location.origin); in local development, it points to http://localhost:3001 configured via Vite's import.meta.env.PROD variable. For QR code functionality, the jsQR library is used for real-time reading from the device camera via a requestAnimationFrame loop, and the qrcode library for generating the QR code as a Data URL encoding the full room access URL.

The Node.js server exports an HTTPS server (in development) or HTTP (in production) attached to a Socket.io instance. Managed socket events include: create (create session), join (join room), start (start game with round configuration), advance (advance to next round), answer (submit player response), end (end game and save scores), close (close session), and rejoin (reconnect to active session). Each event that modifies session state emits a state event to all room clients with the complete updated state, including the server timestamp (serverTime) for clock skew correction.
[Figure 2: Host dashboard showing connected players, answer counter, and advance button]
Figure 2. Host dashboard during an active game with real-time visibility of player status.

Time Machine presents the player with a set of scrambled words that form a Past Simple sentence when correctly ordered. The word bank is generated by randomly shuffling the words of the target sentence. The player taps the words in their preferred order to build the sentence in the answer zone, and can remove or clear selections before verifying. Pressing Verify compares the selection lexicographically with the correct sentence. The sentence bank includes twenty items distributed across five difficulty levels, covering regular and irregular verbs in technology and software development contexts. Difficulty increases based on sentence length and vocabulary complexity.

Duel Mode presents multiple-choice questions with four answer options strictly following four grammatical patterns. For comparatives of short adjectives (one or two syllables), the structure "is ADJer than" is used (e.g., A laptop is bigger than a phone). For comparatives of long adjectives (three or more syllables), "is more ADJ than" is employed (e.g., An SSD is more efficient than an HDD). For superlatives of short adjectives, "the ADJest" is used (e.g., Everest is the tallest mountain). For superlatives of long adjectives, "the most ADJ" is applied (e.g., React is the most popular library). Incorrect options represent the most common student errors: using more with short adjectives, incorrectly forming -er with long adjectives, and confusing comparative with superlative.
[Figure 3: Player view in Duel Mode showing VS cards, sentence with blank, and four answer options]
Figure 3. Player view in the Duel Mode minigame (G2) — Comparatives and Superlatives.

Evidence File follows a reading comprehension format. The player is presented with a short passage written in Present Perfect, featuring constructions such as "has already," "have never," and "has just," in software development team contexts. A comprehension question is then posed requiring the player to correctly identify an action expressed in Present Perfect. Distractors include plausible but incorrect options based on information not present in the passage or on erroneous interpretations. The question bank contains ten items with difficulty levels from one to five.

Crystal Ball presents the player with a contextual situation and a sentence with a blank. The player must select the correct future form from four options: will (spontaneous decisions and general predictions), going to (established plans and evidence-based predictions), won't (negative predictions), and present continuous with future value (confirmed plans with a time or date). The sixteen questions in the bank are distributed across five difficulty levels and cover software project, agile sprint, and systems architecture contexts.

The score for each correct answer is calculated using the following formula, which combines a base component proportional to question difficulty and a speed bonus that rewards answering before time expires:

Where Difficulty takes integer values from 1 to 5. The minimum score per correct answer is Difficulty × 100 points (answered at the last instant) and the maximum is Difficulty × 150 points (answered instantly). For example, a correct answer to a difficulty-5 question answered immediately awards 750 points, while the same answer at the time limit awards 500 points. An incorrect answer or time expiring without a response awards 0 points.

One of the main technical challenges in a multiplayer application with a timer is ensuring that all clients display the same remaining time, regardless of the clock offset between the server and the client device. GrammarX solves this problem by including the serverTime field (server's Date.now()) in every emitted state event. Upon receiving a state event, each client calculates the offset as: clockOffset = client_Date.now() - serverTime. From then on, all timer operations use a synchronized time: syncedNow = Date.now() - clockOffset. The remaining time is calculated as: timeLeft = max(0, timePerRound - (syncedNow - roundStartedAt) / 1000). This mechanism ensures the timer expires simultaneously on all devices with ±200 ms precision, which is the client timer update interval.
[Figure 4: QR code displayed on the host screen encoding the full room access URL]
Figure 4. QR code generated by the application allowing students to join the room directly from their device camera.

GrammarX is deployed on Railway.app, a cloud infrastructure platform that automatically detects Node.js projects and executes the build and start scripts defined in the package.json file. The build process runs vite build to generate the frontend static files in the dist/ directory, which are subsequently served by the Express server in production. The NODE_ENV=production environment variable activates the server's pure HTTP mode, since Railway manages HTTPS termination at the load balancer level. The SQLite database is stored in a persistent volume mounted at the /data directory of the container, configured via the DATA_DIR variable. In the local development environment, the server/start.js script frees ports 3001 and 3002 before starting the server, preventing conflicts with previous instances.

The development of GrammarX resulted in a fully functional multiplayer web application, accessible from any device with a modern browser and internet connection. The application has been successfully deployed on the Railway.app platform and is accessible at https://grammarx-production.up.railway.app/. All functional and non-functional requirements identified in the analysis stage were successfully implemented.

In the classroom context, the application allows the teacher to launch a session in seconds, sharing the room code or QR code with students. Students connect from their smartphones without needing to install any application. The deferred answer reveal mechanic—where the result is only shown once everyone has answered or time expires—creates a moment of suspense that increases interest and participation during the session. The speed-based scoring system incentivizes students to respond with both knowledge and speed, promoting cognitive agility in processing grammatical rules.

The persistent leaderboard provides the teacher with historical information about student performance by minigame, making it possible to identify grammatical areas of greatest difficulty for the group. The four minigames cover the four grammar topics of the English course syllabus progressively in difficulty, from basic beginner-level structures to complex expert-level constructions.

GrammarX successfully consolidated four differentiated grammar learning experiences in a single interactive digital environment, integrating gamification principles that have demonstrated their effectiveness in educational contexts (Kapp, 2012; Prensky, 2001). The use of modern web technologies such as React, Socket.io, and Node.js made it possible to build a robust, low-latency platform accessible from any device, without requiring additional installations by students.

The gamification of grammar learning, when well-designed and aligned with curricular objectives, can transform repetitive exercises into meaningful experiences that motivate students to participate actively. The real-time competition mechanic, combined with immediate feedback (deferred to the reveal moment) and the speed-based scoring system, creates an environment that balances challenge with enjoyment—key factors for effective learning according to Csikszentmihalyi's flow theory.

As future work, the following improvement directions are identified: incorporation of additional grammar topics such as reported speech, passive voice, and conditionals; implementation of individual student profiles with performance history; addition of audio feedback for correct and incorrect answers; support for images in questions; and development of a solo practice mode without requiring a host. These improvements would significantly extend the pedagogical scope of the application and its usefulness in different English teaching contexts.
The following references are presented in APA seventh edition format.
Kapp, K. M. (2012). The gamification of learning and instruction: Game-based methods and strategies for training and education. Pfeiffer.
Meta Platforms. (2024). React – A JavaScript library for building user interfaces. https://react.dev
Prensky, M. (2001). Digital game-based learning. McGraw-Hill.

Wang, A. I., & Lieberoth, A. (2016). The effect of points and audio on concentration, engagement, enjoyment, learning, motivation, and classroom dynamics using Kahoot. In Proceedings of the 10th European Conference on Games Based Learning (pp. 738–746). Academic Conferences International.
